questions.q: Which statement correctly describes a positron?
options: The antiparticle of the electron: same mass as the electron, but positive charge
A particle with twice the electron's mass and positive charge
A proton that has been ejected from the nucleus
A neutral particle emitted alongside beta particles
questions.explanation: A positron is the electron's antiparticle: identical mass (\(9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \mathrm{kg}\)) and equal magnitude of charge, but positive instead of negative. The neutral partner of beta decay (D) is the neutrino.

questions.q: In beta-plus decay, what happens to the atomic number of the nucleus?
options: It increases by 1, because a neutron becomes a proton
It decreases by 1, because a proton becomes a neutron
It stays the same, because only a positron leaves
It decreases by 2, because an alpha particle is emitted
questions.explanation: In beta-plus decay a proton converts to a neutron (emitting a positron and neutrino), so the atomic number falls by 1 while the mass number is unchanged. Beta-MINUS decay (A) is the reverse: a neutron becomes a proton and the atomic number rises.

questions.q: What is the balanced equation for the beta-plus decay of carbon-11?
questions.explanation: Carbon-11 (Z=6) emits a positron, so a proton becomes a neutron and the daughter is boron-11 (Z=5). Mass number stays 11; charge balances: \(6 = 5 + 1\). Option A is beta-minus (wrong direction).

questions.q: When an electron and positron at rest annihilate, why must TWO gamma photons be produced in opposite directions rather than a single photon?
options: To conserve charge, since one photon would be charged
To conserve mass-energy, since one photon could not carry enough energy
To conserve momentum: the pair is at rest (zero total momentum), but a single photon always carries momentum, so two opposite photons are needed to cancel to zero
Because gamma photons can only be created in pairs by the strong force
questions.explanation: Just before annihilation the total momentum is essentially zero. A single photon always carries momentum and can never have zero, so it would violate conservation of momentum. Two equal photons emitted back-to-back have momenta that cancel to zero. Photons are uncharged, so charge (A) is not the issue here.

questions.q: What is the energy of EACH gamma photon produced in electron-positron annihilation at rest?
questions.explanation: Each particle's rest energy is \(m_e c^2 = 0.511\ \mathrm{MeV}\). The total released is \(2m_ec^2 = 1.022\ \mathrm{MeV}\) (option C), shared equally between the two photons, so each carries 0.511 MeV. A common slip is to give 1.022 MeV per photon.

questions.q: How does pair production differ from annihilation in why exactly two particles are involved?
options: Pair production makes two particles to conserve charge; annihilation makes two photons to conserve momentum
Both make two particles to conserve momentum
Pair production makes two photons; annihilation makes two particles
Pair production makes two particles to conserve momentum; annihilation makes two photons to conserve energy
questions.explanation: In pair production a gamma photon becomes an electron and a positron — two particles, one of each charge, to keep total charge zero. In annihilation a pair becomes two photons travelling in opposite directions, needed to conserve momentum. They are reverse processes with different reasons for 'two'.

questions.q: Calculate the total energy released when an electron and positron annihilate at rest, given \(m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \mathrm{kg}\) and \(c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\).
questions.explanation: All the rest mass of both particles converts: \(E = 2m_ec^2 = 2 \times (9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{8})^2 = 1.64 \times 10^{-13}\ \mathrm{J}\). The value \(8.2 \times 10^{-14}\ \mathrm{J}\) (A) is the energy of one of the two photons, half the total.

questions.q: In a PET scan, why does the FDG tracer concentrate in tumours and active brain regions?
options: Because tumours attract positrons electrostatically
Because FDG is a glucose analogue and accumulates in metabolically active tissue that consumes a lot of glucose
Because the magnetic field of the scanner pulls the tracer to active sites
Because gamma photons are absorbed more strongly by active tissue
questions.explanation: FDG behaves like glucose, so it gathers wherever the body uses a lot of glucose — fast-metabolising tissue such as tumours and active brain regions. This is why PET images function (metabolic activity), not just anatomy.

questions.q: In a PET scanner, what is meant by 'coincidence detection' and the 'line of response'?
options: Detecting a single photon and tracing its path backwards through the body
Registering the two annihilation photons simultaneously on opposite sides of the detector ring; the line joining those two detectors passes through the annihilation point
Counting how many decays occur per second to find the tracer's half-life
Detecting the positron directly as it leaves the nucleus
questions.explanation: The two annihilation photons travel in opposite directions and strike the surrounding ring on opposite sides at the same instant (coincidence). Because they came from one event, the annihilation lies on the straight line joining the two detectors — the line of response. Millions of these lines are combined to reconstruct the image.

questions.q: Why is a SHORT-lived positron-emitting isotope (such as fluorine-18, half-life ~110 min) used as a PET tracer?
options: A longer half-life would always give a clearer image, so the shortest possible is best regardless
The half-life must be long enough to image the patient but short enough that the tracer decays and clears quickly, keeping the radiation dose low
Short-lived isotopes emit more photons per decay
Short-lived isotopes are the only ones that emit positrons
questions.explanation: This is the half-life suitability argument: the isotope must last long enough to inject, distribute and scan, but be short-lived enough to clear the body fast and minimise dose. A longer half-life is worse — more dose and slower clearance — so option A is the misconception to avoid.
